topic.title: Uber | OA | SDE intern | On Campus |

post.content: Isn\'t it N^4 time complexity due to substr being used everytime ,isn\u2019t substr O(N)?

Your solution looks correct except for two things.
1. I guess you mean replace in question (ii) otherwise (ii and iii are same (remove and delete))
2. I think it should be min(cost,Edit_Distance(S1.substr(0,i)  ,S1.substr(i,n)  ) ); and not S1.substr(i+1,n-i) in second arg. 

Did your solution go TLE or failed?
What are the constraints on n (the length of the string)?
What\'s the difference between operation 2 and 3?\n\nDo you mean replace a character?

This sounds exactly like Levenshtein distance.  It is commonly used in genomics to measure how similar genome sequences are.  If you search for "Levenshtein distance" on wikipedia, that article has a few approaches for coding the algorithm, including a bottom-up DP solution.
did u get through this round?

Solution 1
```python 
import math

def make_special(string):
    def distance(str1, str2):
        length = abs(len(str1) - len(str2))

        count = math.inf
        for i in range(length + 1):
            if len(str1) < len(str2):
                substr1 = str1
                substr2 = str2[i:i + len(str1)]
            else:
                substr1 = str2
                substr2 = str1[i:i + len(str1)]

            count = min(count, sum(c1 != c2 for c1, c2 in zip(substr1, substr2)) + length)
        return count

    cost = math.inf
    for i in range(1, len(string)):
        str1 = string[:i]
        str2 = string[i:]

        cost = min(cost, distance(str1, str2))

    return cost


string1 = "aabcdeccdecc"
string2 = "cathatratmat"
string3 = "aaxa"
string4 = "xxxx"

print(make_special(string1))
print(make_special(string2))
print(make_special(string3))
print(make_special(string4))
```

Solution 2 following the tip from [@johnfonner](https://leetcode.com/johnfonner) to use Levenshtein algorithm
```python
def make_special(string):
    def levenshtein(str1, str2):
        distance = [[0] * len(str2) for _ in range(len(str1))]

        for i in range(len(str1)):
            distance[i][0] = i
        for j in range(len(str2)):
            distance[0][j] = j

        for i in range(len(str1) - 1):
            for j in range(len(str2) - 1):
                edit = 1
                if str1[i] == str2[j]:
                    edit = 0
                distance[i+1][j+1] = min(distance[i][j+1] + 1, distance[i+1][j] + 1, distance[i][j] + edit)
        return distance[len(str1) - 1][len(str2) - 1]

    cost = math.inf
    for i in range(1, len(string)):
        str1 = string[:i]
        str2 = string[i:]

        cost = min(cost, levenshtein(str1, str2))

    return cost


string1 = "aabcdeccdecc"
string2 = "cathatratmat"
string3 = "aaxa"
string4 = "xxxx"

print(make_special(string1))
print(make_special(string2))
print(make_special(string3))
print(make_special(string4))
```

but correct me if I'm wrong, but both solutions take O(N^3) for time complexity and the second solution O(N/2^2) for space (N being the size of the string)

I think the idea of using the edit distance algorithm is correct, but I believe it should be done in the following manner by splitting it into cases:
  1) if the length is 1, just return 1 because you can append the same character to the end of the string and it will fit the constraints
  2) otherwise, let's take the length of the string as N:
       -> take the first ceiling(N / 2) characters of the string (call this first half A), and take the remaining floor(N / 2) characters (call this second half B)
       -> call edit distance on A and B, and then return that as the final answer. (Side note: if the length of the string is odd, maybe also try to run the edit distance algorithm again but this time the string A is defined as the first floor(N / 2) and the string B is defined as the remaining ceiling(N / 2) and return the better of the 2 answers between the two edit distance calls. I'm not sure if this would make a difference to the answer, but it would be easy to do and the solution would still run in O(N^2) time.)

(this solution is assuming the length of the string is between 1 and 5000 inclusive as the OP has mentioned in a comment under this thread)
The complexity of this solution is O(N^2).

Someone let me know if this solution is incorrect.

EDIT: [@Archit-Bikram](https://leetcode.com/Archit-Bikram) gave a counter example for this solution. So, unfortunately, this solution does not work.
